planting.note: Plant after frost into warm, fertile soil.

directions: Plant tubers four to six inches deep in rich, moist soil in part sun to bright shade. The broad chartreuse-to-lime leaves glow brightest with some morning sun and afternoon shelter. Keep evenly watered and feed through summer for vivid color and quick size.

planting.note: Set rhizomes out after the soil has warmed and frost has passed.

directions: Plant the rhizomes horizontally about two to three inches deep in rich, moist soil with the growing tips pointed upward. Keep the bed consistently damp through summer and feed monthly for lush canes. Fragrant white flowers open at the top of the stalks from late summer into fall; cut spent stems to the ground after frost in colder zones.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring once nights stay reliably above freezing.

directions: Bury the rhizomes a couple of inches below the surface in fertile, moisture-retentive ground. Water freely during active growth and mulch the crowns heavily where winters are marginal. Slender spikes of coral to salmon-red flowers with long stamens appear in late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in warm spring soil.

directions: Set the rhizomes just below the soil surface in moist, fertile ground with afternoon shade in hot climates. Keep well watered and fed for tall leafy canes. Highly fragrant pale-yellow flowers open in autumn, often as the season's last ginger to bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this species is among the hardier ginger lilies.

directions: Plant rhizomes two inches deep in well-drained but moisture-retentive soil in sun to light shade. This is one of the earlier and hardier gingers, tolerating a deep winter mulch in borderline zones. Dense narrow spikes of small orange flowers appear in mid to late summer.

planting.note: A hardier selection that can be planted a little earlier with mulch protection.

directions: Plant the rhizomes a couple inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil and mulch the crown well for winter in cooler zones. Water steadily through summer growth. Bold spikes of soft orange flowers with red stamens open reliably in late summer and hold for weeks.

planting.note: Plant in warm spring soil after frost.

directions: Plant rhizomes shallowly in rich, moist soil where the cream-streaked variegated foliage gets bright but not scorching light. Keep it well watered and fed for the best leaf color. Fragrant peach-orange flowers appear atop the canes in late summer.

planting.note: Set out divisions after the last frost.

directions: Plant rhizomes two inches deep in deep, fertile soil with steady moisture. This vigorous hybrid throws tall canes, so give it room and shelter from wind. Large fragrant coral-pink flower heads open from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions once soil has warmed in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizomes just under the surface in rich, moist ground in partial shade. Water generously and feed through the growing season. Fragrant soft-yellow flowers with a warm apricot flush open in late summer and early fall.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Plant rhizomes shallowly in moist, humus-rich soil where the maroon-backed foliage is shown off. Keep it damp and warm through the season. Orange-red flowers appear in summer, and the plant readily forms bulbils on the stems that can be potted up.

planting.note: One of the hardier species; plant in spring with winter mulch in cool zones.

directions: Plant rhizomes two inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. This mountain species tolerates cooler conditions and a deep winter mulch. Loose spikes of fragrant white flowers with orange-red bases open in late summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers once the soil is warm in late spring.

directions: Plant the tuberous roots about two inches deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil and keep warm and damp to break dormancy. Water freely once growth begins but ease off as the plant goes dormant in fall. Tulip-like pink bracts rise on slim stalks through summer and last for weeks.

planting.note: Plant tubers into warm soil in late spring.

directions: Set tubers two inches deep in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and keep consistently warm and moist. Feed lightly during active growth for the strongest flower stalks. Bright rose-pink bracts appear over summer, excellent as a long-lasting cut flower.

planting.note: Plant tubers in warm spring soil after frost.

directions: Plant tubers two to three inches deep in rich, moist soil in dappled shade. Keep well watered through summer while the broad tropical leaves develop. The cone-shaped pink and green flower heads emerge low, often tucked among the foliage, which gives the plant its name.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, rich soil.

directions: Plant tubers a few inches deep in fertile, moist soil in bright shade to show off the white-margined leaves. Water steadily and avoid harsh midday sun that can scorch the variegation. Pink flower cones appear low among the striking foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers into warm soil in late spring.

directions: Plant tubers two inches deep in rich, well-drained soil kept evenly moist and warm. Provide bright dappled light and steady feeding. Brilliant orange, waxy flower cones rise above the pleated leaves through summer.

planting.note: Plant large tubers in warm spring soil; blooms often precede the leaves.

directions: Plant the big tubers three inches deep in deep, rich, moist soil with room to spread. Water heavily during the growing season for its massive banana-like leaves. Tall pink and green flower plumes emerge in spring, frequently before the foliage fully expands.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in warm spring soil after frost.

directions: Plant rhizomes two inches deep in rich, moist soil in bright dappled shade to keep the cream-edged foliage crisp. Water freely and shelter from harsh sun. Green pinecone flower heads that redden with age appear in summer among the striking variegated leaves.

planting.note: One of the hardier gingers; plant in spring with a winter mulch in cool zones.

directions: Plant rhizomes two inches deep in rich, moist, shaded soil where it can slowly spread into a clump. Keep evenly moist through summer. Pale creamy-yellow flowers emerge at ground level in late summer, and the young flower buds and shoots are edible.

planting.note: Tropical only; plant divisions where it stays frost-free.

directions: Plant divisions in deep, rich, moist soil in a warm sheltered site with bright light. Keep the soil wet and feed regularly during growth. Large soft-pink bract spikes top the canes and make excellent long-lasting cut flowers.

planting.note: Plant divisions in warm soil after frost.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich soil in bright shade; the crushed foliage smells strongly of cardamom. Keep evenly moist and shelter from frost. Nodding clusters of white shell-like flowers appear on older canes in warm climates, though it is often grown mainly for its aromatic leaves.

planting.note: Plant sprouted rhizome pieces in warm soil after frost.

directions: Plant plump rhizome sections with visible buds an inch or two deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil in warmth and dappled light. Keep evenly moist and feed through the long growing season. Flowering is uncommon in cultivation, but the aromatic rhizomes can be dug once the leaves begin to die back in fall.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors after all frost danger passes.

directions: Plant divisions in fertile, well-drained soil where the maroon-blotched leaves catch bright light to deepen their color. Keep the roots consistently moist and shelter the foliage from strong wind that shreds the blades. Small pink-tinged flower bracts appear on established clumps, followed by tiny inedible fruit.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings outdoors once nights stay warm.

directions: Sow scarified seed in warm compost and keep it evenly moist while germination trickles in. Grow in fertile soil with generous feeding and moisture for the best display. A slender upright inflorescence with lavender-pink bracts rises from the crown on plants a year or more old.

planting.note: Set out established plants after the last frost.

directions: Plant in deep, rich soil where young leaves show their red-streaked markings best in strong light. Water and feed heavily in summer, then mulch the crown thickly for winter in borderline zones. Mature clumps produce a large drooping flower spike after a season or two of vigorous growth.

planting.note: Plant out or pot up after frost has passed.

directions: Set the pup or corm in rich, free-draining soil and keep it warm, fed, and watered through the growing season. Its compact height makes it a good container or greenhouse subject that can be moved under cover for winter. A large purple flower bud emerges and unfurls to reveal hands of small sweet fruit on well-grown plants.

planting.note: Plant divisions out in spring after frost.

directions: Plant this stocky dwarf banana relative in well-drained, fertile soil with full sun and shelter from cold wind. It is hardier than most bananas and the crown will resprout after mulching in milder temperate gardens. A long-lasting golden bract cluster shaped like a lotus opens at the top of the pseudostem on mature plants.

planting.note: Transplant seed-grown plants outdoors after frost.

directions: Sow the hard round seed warm and keep it moist while it germinates over several weeks. Grow the swollen, waxy blue-green pseudostem in rich soil with plenty of water and full sun. It is a single-stemmed foliage plant that flowers only after several years, so treat it as a bold structural accent.

planting.note: Plant out after frost where space allows a large clump.

directions: Sow soaked seed warm and be patient through slow germination. Give this tree-sized species room, well-drained soil, and shelter from wind that tatters the large banana-like leaves. Bold white-and-blue flowers held in dark boat-shaped bracts appear high on mature, multi-trunked clumps.

planting.note: Plant out divisions after frost; keep slightly root-restricted for bloom.

directions: This yellow-flowered selection is grown from division to stay true to color. Plant in well-drained soil and allow the roots to fill their space, which promotes flowering. The golden-yellow blooms with a blue tongue open on established clumps, mostly through the cool season.

planting.note: Set out large plants after frost where a tree-form clump can grow.

directions: Sow soaked seed in warm compost and grow the seedlings on steadily; they are slow to reach flowering size. This tree-like species needs space, rich well-drained soil, and protection from strong wind. Large all-white flowers emerge from a single dark bract on mature trunks.

planting.note: Transplant seed-grown plants outdoors after frost.

directions: Sow the soaked seed warm and grow the young plants on for several years to reach maturity. Give this tall, single-trunked to sparingly branched species well-drained soil and full sun. Its white flowers resemble those of the giant white bird of paradise and carry a distinctive threadlike appendage on the bract.

planting.note: Start seed warm and plant out after frost.

directions: Soak the seed and sow warm, allowing weeks for uneven germination. Grow this vigorous species in deep, rich soil with heavy watering and feeding through the season. A large maroon flower bud emerges and bends downward, producing seedy fruit; it is grown mainly for its stately tropical foliage.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; mulch crowns for winter in cool zones.

directions: Sow the soaked seed warm and grow the seedlings on in rich, moist soil. This species spreads by underground runners and is among the more cold-tolerant bananas, resprouting after a mulched winter in milder temperate gardens. Established clumps throw a pendent flower spike after a full season, followed by small seedy fruit.

planting.note: Move outdoors once frost danger has fully passed.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into humus-rich soil and mulch to hold moisture. This compact selection shows green leaves striped with cream and edged in pink-red, colors that intensify with brighter light. Enjoy it chiefly for the layered, variegated foliage rosettes.

planting.note: Plant in spring so roots establish before its first winter outdoors.

directions: Sow fresh seed shallowly in a gritty mix, or plant a young rosette at its original depth in free-draining soil. Tolerates wind, coastal salt, and light frost once established, but protect young plants in cold snaps. Mature specimens send up large, sweetly fragrant creamy flower panicles in early summer.

planting.note: Establish in spring in a sheltered, sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant in free-draining soil raised slightly if drainage is poor, since wet winter roots cause rot. The narrow arching leaves hold a deep bronze-red that reads strongest in full sun. Grown as a dramatic focal accent for its dark spiky foliage rather than its eventual white flower spikes.

planting.note: Plant out in spring after hard frosts end.

directions: Plant at its nursery depth in sharp-draining soil and keep crowns free of standing water over winter. The chocolate-burgundy leaves carry bright pink margins that glow when backlit. Use it as a vertical accent in beds or large containers for the vivid striped foliage.

planting.note: Best planted in spring in a warm, partly shaded position.

directions: Root cane cuttings in warm moist mix, then plant into fertile soil with light overhead shade. This clumping species forms tall slender canes topped with thin dark-green leaves. In spring it bears loose sprays of small lilac-purple flowers followed by dark berries.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring for the best establishment.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained soil with the crown just at the surface. Full sun deepens the smoky purple-bronze coloring of the erect leaves. Remove any all-green reverting shoots to keep the dark foliage true.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot with good drainage.

directions: Set divisions at crown level in fertile, free-draining soil and water through the first summer. The arching leaves are chartreuse-yellow with green margins, gently drooping for a fountain effect. Give some afternoon shade in hot climates to prevent leaf scorch on the pale foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring so the clump matures before cold weather.

directions: Plant divisions upright with the crown at soil level in sharply draining ground. The upright olive-bronze leaves carry a distinct rose-pink edge that brightens in strong light. Grown for the tall striped foliage that anchors mixed borders and large pots.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Plant divisions at their original depth and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. This mid-sized selection shows pink-and-green twisting leaves that arch outward. Its compact habit suits containers and the front of borders where the bicolor foliage stands out.

planting.note: Plant in spring; its small size makes it easy to pot and overwinter.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at soil level in gritty, well-drained soil. The narrow deep-bronze leaves arch gracefully and hold their color best in full sun. A dependable dwarf flax for containers and small-space plantings.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, well-drained position.

directions: Plant divisions at crown level and keep roots on the dry side over winter. The broad upright leaves blend bronze-green centers with rosy-pink and cream margins that warm as they age. Valued as a large accent for its softly colored, upright foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to develop the darkest color.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at the surface in well-drained soil. The upright leaves are near-black purple, holding a glossy sheen in strong light. A striking dark-foliage flax for contrast in beds and containers.

planting.note: Plant or sow in spring in free-draining ground.

directions: Sow seed shallowly or plant divisions with the crown at soil level. Smaller and more arching than common flax, it tolerates wind and coastal exposure well. Mature clumps carry greenish-yellow flowers on curving stems followed by twisted hanging seed pods.

planting.note: Keep above 50 F; container culture allows indoor overwintering.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in a light, well-drained mix and provide bright indirect light. The leaves are striped green, cream, and red, giving an overall pinkish cast that is best under good light. Water sparingly and grow it for the colorful arching foliage crown.

planting.note: Outdoors only in tropical zones; elsewhere a durable houseplant.

directions: Root thick cane sections upright in warm moist mix, then pot into well-drained soil. It tolerates low light but grows best in bright, filtered light with even moisture. On mature plants, panicles of intensely fragrant creamy flowers occasionally open at night.

planting.note: Keep warm and frost-free; commonly grown indoors.

directions: Plant rooted canes in a free-draining mix and site in bright indirect light. Each arching leaf carries a broad golden-green central stripe that fades in deep shade. Use distilled or filtered water to avoid the tip burn this selection is prone to.

planting.note: Grow indoors or in frost-free shade gardens.

directions: Root cane cuttings and pot into a loose, well-drained mix. This selection has glossy solid dark-green leaves and tolerates lower light better than most. Keep evenly moist but never waterlogged, and wipe leaves to keep them shining.

planting.note: Keep warm and out of frost; a reliable indoor plant.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in a fast-draining mix in bright indirect light. The upright sword leaves are gray-green with crisp white and green longitudinal stripes. Let the surface dry slightly between waterings and grow it for the striped upright foliage.

planting.note: Frost-tender; grow as a houseplant outside the tropics.

directions: Root tip cuttings in warm moist mix and pot into well-drained soil. The short recurved leaves are edged in bright chartreuse-yellow that stays vivid in good light. Grown for the whorled, gold-margined foliage on its slowly branching stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, very well-drained spot; tolerates only light frost.

directions: Soak and sow seed in a gritty mix at warm temperatures, expecting slow, uneven germination. Give it lean, sharply draining soil and full sun, and water sparingly as it stores moisture in its thick trunk. This slow, long-lived tree eventually forms an umbrella crown and bleeds red 'dragon's blood' resin when cut.

planting.note: Grown indoors almost everywhere; keep well above freezing.

directions: Root cane cuttings in a few inches of water or in moist, well-drained soil. In water culture use filtered or distilled water and change it regularly to prevent tip yellowing and rot. Grow it in bright indirect light for the upright green stems and modest strap leaves.

planting.note: Tender; grow as a shade houseplant outside the tropics.

directions: Root cuttings in warm moist mix and pot into rich, well-drained soil. Unlike its strappy relatives, it bears broad oval leaves speckled with creamy-gold spots on wiry stems. Keep in low to moderate light and evenly moist for the best-spotted foliage.

planting.note: Establish in spring in a sunny, sheltered, well-drained site.

directions: Plant in free-draining soil, raising the crown slightly where winters are wet. The arching leaves are green with broad creamy-white margins that brighten a sunny border. Grow it as a bold variegated accent rather than for its later scented white flower plumes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at soil level and avoid winter waterlogging. The arching bronze-red leaves are striped with rosy-pink margins that intensify in full sun. Grown as a colorful upright accent for beds and large containers.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, well-drained position.

directions: Plant divisions at crown level in gritty, free-draining soil. The gracefully arching leaves are deep burgundy-red, glowing when backlit by low sun. A compact red-foliage flax well suited to containers and border edges.

planting.note: Set plants out only after all danger of frost has passed and nights stay above 50F.

directions: Root softwood cuttings in moist, well-drained mix or set out nursery plants at the same depth they grew in the pot. Feed regularly through the warm season and keep soil evenly moist but never waterlogged. Blooms flush from late spring through fall, with each flower lasting only a day or two.

planting.note: Plant outdoors once soil warms and frost risk is gone.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil in a warm, sheltered spot with plenty of sun. Pinch tips in early growth to build a bushy frame and water deeply during dry spells. Large single scarlet flowers open across the warm months.

planting.note: A vigorous old cultivar; set out after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Give this large grower room and a full-sun position with well-drained soil. It stands up well as a hedge or specimen and tolerates hard pruning to keep its size in check. Single bright red flowers with a golden staminal column appear almost year-round in frost-free climates.

planting.note: Plant out in late spring once nights are reliably warm.

directions: Set in fertile, well-drained soil and shelter from strong wind that can shred the large blooms. Keep evenly moist and feed lightly but often during active growth. Ruffled apricot-orange flowers carry a deep red throat and bloom through the warm season.

planting.note: A collector hybrid best grown in a pot that can be moved indoors when cold.

directions: Grow in a container of free-draining mix so the roots stay warm and never soggy. Provide bright light and steady feeding to fuel repeat flowering. Multi-toned blooms blending yellow, pink, and lavender open singly and last a day or two.

planting.note: Prized for flowers where no two are exactly alike; set out after frost.

directions: Plant in warm, well-drained soil and give it consistent moisture and feeding. Shelter the blooms from harsh sun and wind to keep the colors crisp. Speckled and streaked flowers in mixed pink, red, and white hues appear all season.

planting.note: A popular exotic-fancy cultivar grown in pots in cooler zones.

directions: Use a rich but fast-draining potting mix and keep the plant in bright, warm conditions. Water when the top inch dries and feed regularly through the growing season. Large ruffled flowers blend yellow, pink, and lavender tones and open across the warm months.

planting.note: A large-flowered fancy hybrid; move under cover before frost.

directions: Grow in a container of open, well-drained mix and keep roots warm and evenly moist. Feed lightly and often to support the heavy flowering habit. Very large blooms in blended pastel and warm tones appear one at a time through the season.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a warm, sunny, sheltered spot.

directions: Set in fertile, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist during the growing season. Prune lightly after flushes to maintain a tidy, bushy shape. Large soft-pink to near-white flowers with a red eye open across the warm months.

planting.note: A classic double-flowered cultivar; set out once nights stay warm.

directions: Plant in warm, well-drained soil in full sun and water consistently through summer. Feed with a balanced fertilizer to keep the heavy double blooms coming. Fully double golden-yellow flowers flushed with orange open across the warm season.

planting.note: An old, reliable double form; plant out after frost.

directions: Give it full sun and rich, well-drained soil, watering deeply in dry weather. Pinch young growth to encourage branching and more flowering wood. Rosette-like double red flowers appear steadily through the warm months.

planting.note: A tough, free-flowering landscape pink; plant after frost.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil where it can grow into a large, dependable shrub. Water regularly while young and prune to shape after the main flushes. Bright rose-pink single flowers bloom heavily and almost continuously in warm climates.

planting.note: A vigorous, cold-tolerant favorite; set out in late spring.

directions: Plant in a warm, sunny spot with well-drained soil and steady moisture. This cultivar flowers freely and holds its blooms well, responding to regular feeding. Large canary-yellow flowers with a deep red center open through the warm season.

planting.note: A classic double-flowered Hawaiian-type cultivar; plant after frost.

directions: Grow in rich, well-drained soil in full sun with regular water and feeding. Support the branches if the heavy double flowers weigh them down. Full double pink flowers with ruffled petals open across the warm months.

planting.note: A large-flowered blend cultivar; set out once frost has passed.

directions: Plant in warm, well-drained soil and provide bright light for the best color. Keep evenly moist and feed regularly to sustain repeat blooming. Large single flowers blend orange, pink, and yellow with a glowing center through the season.

planting.note: A compact, floriferous white; good for containers and small beds.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in a warm, sunny location and water consistently. Its naturally bushy, compact habit needs only light pruning to stay neat. Clean white single flowers with a soft pale center bloom freely all season.

planting.note: Named for its crepe-textured petals; plant out after frost.

directions: Set in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun with regular deep watering. It grows into a large shrub and tolerates pruning to keep it in bounds. Ruffled, crinkled pink flowers with a crepe-paper texture open through the warm season.

planting.note: A large single white with narrow petals; set out after frost.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, giving this vigorous grower ample room. Water deeply during dry periods and prune to control its size. Slender-petaled white flowers with a small red center appear almost year-round in frost-free climates.

planting.note: A showy exotic-type hybrid; grow in a pot in cooler zones.

directions: Grow in a rich, fast-draining mix in a warm, bright position sheltered from wind. Water when the surface dries and feed regularly during active growth. Very large ruffled flowers blend pink, lavender, and cream tones through the season.

planting.note: An unusual smoky-toned collector cultivar; keep frost-free.

directions: Grow in a container of open, well-drained mix and keep the roots warm year-round. Provide bright light and steady feeding to bring out the muted colors. Single flowers in dusky lavender, gray, and tan tones open one at a time through the warm season.

planting.note: Known for very large multi-colored blooms; plant after frost.

directions: Plant in warm, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun with consistent moisture. Feed regularly to support the very large flowers, and shelter them from wind. Huge single blooms blend peach, orange, coral, and lavender through the season.

planting.note: A richly colored fancy hybrid; grow in a pot where cold threatens.

directions: Use a warm, bright spot and a well-drained potting mix kept evenly moist. Feed lightly and often to keep the deeply saturated flowers coming. Single blooms in blended red, orange, and pink tones open across the warm season.

planting.note: A distinctive tiered 'hat' or parasol-type flower; keep frost-free.

directions: Grow in warm, well-drained soil or a container in bright light with steady water. Feed during active growth to support its unusual two-story flowers. Red blooms carry a raised central column tipped with a smaller secondary flower.

planting.note: Grown mainly for variegated foliage; set out after frost.

directions: Plant in full sun to bring out the strongest leaf coloring in well-drained soil. Water evenly and pinch tips to keep the plant dense and well-branched. The narrow leaves are marbled cream, pink, and green, with occasional small red flowers.

planting.note: A bright single red; plant out once nights are warm.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, watering deeply in dry weather. Prune after flushes to keep it compact and encourage new flowering wood. Vivid single red flowers open freely across the warm months.

planting.note: A soft yellow single; plant after frost in full sun.

directions: Set in warm, well-drained soil in full sun and keep evenly moist through the season. Feed with a balanced fertilizer to sustain steady bloom production. Clear yellow single flowers with a paler center open across the warm months.

planting.note: A bicolor blend hybrid; grow in a pot where frost occurs.

directions: Grow in a warm, bright position in well-drained soil kept evenly moist. Feed regularly during active growth for the best flower color and size. Single blooms brush pink and yellow tones with a red central eye through the season.

planting.note: Part of the Tahitian series of warm-toned hybrids; keep frost-free.

directions: Grow in well-drained soil or a container in a warm, sunny, sheltered spot. Keep evenly moist and feed lightly but often through the growing season. Large single flowers glow in orange and coral tones with a contrasting center all season.

planting.note: A brightly patterned single; plant after frost in full sun.

directions: Plant in warm, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun with steady watering. Feed regularly and shelter the blooms from strong wind. Single flowers in vivid pink and orange tones with a bright eye open across the warm months.

planting.note: A vigorous landscape single; set out once frost risk has passed.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, allowing room for a large shrub. Water deeply during dry spells and prune to maintain shape and size. Single flowers in warm orange-red tones bloom heavily through the season.

planting.note: Pot up rooted offsets in spring once nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Set the offset in a fast-draining bark-based mix with the base just firm, not buried deep. Keep the central cup filled with rainwater or distilled water and let the potting mix approach dryness between waterings. The silver-banded rosette pushes a pink bract with blue flowers after two to three years, then produces pups to replace it.

planting.note: Divide and pot offsets in warm spring weather.

directions: Grow this selected form in loose, airy mix and give it bright indirect light for the best silver scurf on the leaves. Water into the central cup and flush it every few weeks to prevent stagnation. A large rose-pink flower head rises after two or more years of growth.

planting.note: Mount or pot offsets in spring in a warm, humid spot.

directions: Plant in coarse epiphyte mix or mount on bark, keeping humidity high and air moving. The banded silver-and-green leaves surround a branched orange-and-red inflorescence that appears on mature rosettes. Keep the cup topped with clean water and feed lightly during warm months.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in full sun for the brightest leaf color.

directions: Give this large landscape bromeliad bright sun to turn the foliage coppery orange and gold. Plant in well-drained soil or a raised bed and water at the base and cup during dry spells. A tall branched yellow flower spike tops mature clumps, which spread widely by offsets.

planting.note: Pot up clumps of offsets in spring.

directions: Grow the strappy green rosettes in a loose, well-drained mix and keep them evenly moist in warm weather. Spikes of blue-tipped pink flowers that look like lit matchsticks rise above the foliage in fall and winter. Divide crowded clumps after flowering to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Mount or pot offsets in spring in bright light.

directions: This compact species tolerates brighter, drier conditions than many bromeliads, blushing red at the center as it prepares to bloom. Grow in very well-drained mix or mounted on wood and water sparingly. A short rose-pink flower cluster nestles in the colored heart of the rosette.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in a warm, bright location.

directions: Set the tubular rosette in fast-draining epiphyte mix and keep the cup filled with clean water. A red flower spike carrying yellow blooms rises from mature plants, contrasting with the dark, sometimes banded leaves. Offsets form freely at the base after flowering.

planting.note: Divide and pot pups in warm spring weather.

directions: This robust hybrid forms a green urn topped by a striking branched inflorescence of blue and coral bracts that lasts for months. Grow in loose, well-drained mix with bright indirect light and keep the central cup filled. Remove spent rosettes once pups are well established.

planting.note: Pot rooted pups in spring in a warm, humid spot.

directions: Grow this soft-leaved bromeliad in bright indirect light out of hot sun, which scorches the foliage. Keep the central cup filled with low-mineral water and the bark mix lightly moist. A long-lasting star of scarlet bracts opens from the center of mature rosettes.

planting.note: Divide pups in spring and pot in airy mix.

directions: This popular hybrid produces a broad head of yellow-to-orange bracts above glossy green leaves. Keep it in warm, humid, bright indirect light and refresh the water in the cup regularly. The colorful bract head holds for many weeks before the plant offsets.

planting.note: Pot offsets in spring in warm conditions.

directions: Grow this tall hybrid for its bold red bract spike carried well above the leafy rosette. Provide bright filtered light, high humidity, and clean water in the central cup. The inflorescence stays showy for a month or more before pups take over.

planting.note: Plant pups in spring in bright, humid conditions.

directions: Unlike most bromeliads, this species turns its whole flat rosette brilliant red as it flowers rather than sending up a tall spike. Grow in bright indirect light with steady warmth and humidity. Small white-and-yellow flowers sit low in the crimson center at bloom time.

planting.note: Pot pups in spring in a warm, shaded spot.

directions: Prized for foliage banded with fine snakeskin-like markings, this species wants warm, humid shade and gentle light. Keep the cup filled with clean water and the mix barely moist. A club-shaped head of red bracts and yellow flowers rises from mature plants.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in warm, humid shade.

directions: This striking species carries a tight cone of orange-red bracts tipped with black at the top of a tall stalk. Give it bright filtered light, warmth, and steady humidity. Keep the tank cup topped with low-mineral water throughout the growing season.

planting.note: Pot pups in spring in warm, humid shade.

directions: Grow the dark-banded rosette in bright indirect light and keep the central cup filled with clean water. A flat, sword-shaped spike of brilliant red bracts rises well above the leaves at maturity. The bract blade stays colorful for weeks while small yellow flowers emerge from its edges.

planting.note: Divide pups in spring and pot in airy mix.

directions: This compact species produces a flattened, branched spike of red-and-yellow bracts resembling a lobster claw. Grow in soft light with warmth and humidity, keeping the cup filled with low-mineral water. The bright bracts last for weeks before the rosette declines and offsets.

planting.note: Pot pups in spring in warm, humid shade.

directions: Grown mainly for its large leaves marked with dark hieroglyphic-like crossbands, this species forms an impressive specimen rosette. Give it bright filtered light and steady warmth and keep the cup filled with clean water. A tall branched flower spike appears only on well-established mature plants.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in warm, bright shade.

directions: This large foliage bromeliad shows wavy purple-brown crossbands across broad pale leaves. Grow in bright indirect light with warmth and humidity for the strongest markings. Keep the central cup filled and let the mix dry slightly between waterings.

planting.note: Pot pups in spring in warm, humid conditions.

directions: This florist hybrid sends up a flat, feather-shaped spike of glowing red-and-yellow bracts above a smooth green rosette. Give it bright indirect light, warmth, and clean water in the cup. The colorful bract blade holds for a month or more indoors.

planting.note: Divide pups in spring and pot in airy mix.

directions: This variegated form carries leaves striped in cream, green, and pink that blush red at the center before flowering. Bright indirect light keeps the stripes crisp and the blush strong. Keep the cup filled with clean water and remove old rosettes after pups form.

planting.note: Pot or mount the small pups in spring.

directions: This miniature hybrid turns fiery red all over in bright light and quickly forms cascading clumps on long stolons. Grow it potted, in a hanging basket, or mounted on wood with good light and airflow. Tiny purple flowers appear in the cups of mature rosettes.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in bright, warm conditions.

directions: Each leaf tip is capped with a bright red spot like a painted fingernail, and the leaf undersides show fine gray banding. Grow in bright indirect light and keep the central cup filled with clean water. Small blue flowers open low in the cup as the plant matures.

planting.note: Pot pups in spring in warm, bright light.

directions: This broad, flat rosette develops a deep purple-flushed center marked with dark spots and cross-bands. Bright indirect light brings out the coloring while protecting the leaves from scorch. Clusters of violet flowers sit in the darkened cup at bloom time.

planting.note: Divide pups in spring and pot in airy mix.

directions: This hybrid forms a glossy rosette in rich red and maroon tones that deepen under strong light. Grow in bright indirect light with good airflow and keep the cup filled with clean water. Small pale flowers appear in the cup as the plant reaches maturity.

planting.note: Pot offsets in spring in bright, warm light.

directions: Broad green leaves flush coppery red at the center as this rosette approaches flowering. Give it bright indirect light and keep the central cup topped with clean water. Small blue-violet flowers open low in the colored heart of the plant.

planting.note: Divide crowded clumps in spring and pot the offsets.

directions: This tough, clumping bromeliad tolerates cooler conditions than most and forms grassy rosettes quickly. Grow in well-drained mix with bright light and keep it evenly moist during the growing season. Arching stems of nodding pink-and-green flowers with blue-edged petals appear in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Divide and pot offsets in spring.

directions: This vigorous, easy bromeliad forms dense clumps of upright green rosettes. Grow in bright light with well-drained mix and keep it lightly moist in warm weather. A short, dense pyramid of bright red flowers tipped with blue rises briefly from the center in late summer or fall.

planting.note: Pot offsets in spring in bright, warm light.

directions: Tall tubular rosettes are banded with silver zebra stripes across bronzy-green leaves. Grow in bright light and well-drained mix, letting it dry somewhat between waterings. A pendent flower spike with pink bracts and coppery petals dangles from mature plants.

planting.note: Divide pups in spring and pot in airy mix.

directions: This classic hybrid forms tall tubular rosettes in deep maroon-red, dusted with silvery scurf and pale spots. Bright light keeps the wine coloring strong. A drooping spike of pink bracts and blue-tipped flowers hangs from the center of mature plants.

planting.note: Pot offsets in spring in bright, warm light.

directions: Leaves of this hybrid are marbled maroon and green and splashed with irregular cream and pink flecks. Grow in bright indirect light and well-drained mix, allowing slight drying between waterings. A pendent spike of pink bracts and blue-edged flowers emerges from mature rosettes.

planting.note: Divide pups in spring and pot in airy mix.

directions: This tall hybrid carries a long, pendent branched spike of violet-blue flowers over a green urn. Grow in bright indirect light with a loose, well-drained mix and keep the cup filled. The dangling flower spray is long-lasting before the rosette produces offsets.

planting.note: Pot offsets in spring in bright, warm light.

directions: Broad green leaves are marbled and blotched with reddish-purple markings that deepen in strong light. Grow in bright indirect light and keep the central cup filled with clean water. Small pale-blue flowers open low in the cup on mature rosettes.

planting.note: Divide pups in spring and pot in airy mix.

directions: This florist hybrid produces a flat, sword-like spike of bright red bracts above a smooth green rosette. Give it bright indirect light, warmth, and clean water in the central cup. Small yellow flowers emerge along the bract edges over several weeks.

planting.note: Pot pups in spring in warm, humid conditions.

directions: This hybrid carries a rounded head of glowing red bracts above glossy green foliage. Provide bright filtered light, warmth, and high humidity, refreshing the water in the cup regularly. The colorful bract head stays showy for many weeks before pups form.

planting.note: Divide pups in spring and pot in airy mix.

directions: This showy hybrid forms tall silvery rosettes heavily marbled with pink, lavender, and cream. Bright light strengthens the coloring and keeps the plant compact. A large pendent spike of pink bracts and blue-tipped flowers hangs from mature rosettes.

planting.note: Pot divisions in spring once indoor warmth is steady.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in a loose, bark-rich mix and keep it in bright indirect light out of cold drafts. Water when the top inch dries and mist or run a humidifier to hold humidity high. The glossy red, heart-shaped spathes appear on and off through the year, each lasting many weeks before fading.

planting.note: Repot or divide in spring as new growth resumes.

directions: Plant in an airy, well-drained mix and give it warm, bright indirect light. Let the surface dry slightly between waterings and keep humidity up around the leaves. Look for the curled, pigtail-like orange spadix rising above a bright red spathe over a long bloom season.

planting.note: Divide or repot in spring as growth resumes.

directions: Set in a fertile, moisture-retentive mix and grow in bright indirect to low light away from cold windows. Keep the mix evenly moist but never waterlogged, and wipe the broad leaves to keep them glossy. This popular large selection produces generous flushes of showy white spathes.

planting.note: Repot or divide the large clump in spring.

directions: Give this giant selection a roomy pot of rich, well-drained mix and bright indirect or medium light. Keep the soil consistently moist and provide humidity to support the very large, deeply ribbed leaves. Big white spathes emerge above the foliage, mainly in the warmer months.

planting.note: Divide in spring, keeping variegated shoots intact.

directions: Grow in a rich, well-drained mix in medium to bright indirect light, which keeps the white streaking on the leaves strong. Water when the surface begins to dry and maintain moderate humidity. This variegated selection sends up the familiar white spathes above cream-splashed green foliage.

planting.note: Take stem cuttings in the warm season to multiply plants.

directions: Root nodal cuttings and grow in a well-drained mix in bright indirect light, which keeps the lime-green center stripe vivid. Water when the top inch dries and pinch stems to encourage bushier growth. Grown for its variegated heart-shaped foliage on trailing vines.

planting.note: Propagate from stem cuttings in spring or summer.

directions: Root a cutting with a healthy node and pot it into a loose, well-drained mix under bright indirect light. Allow the surface to dry slightly between waterings to avoid rot. Loved for its soft, velvety heart-shaped leaves that show bronze and purple tones in good light.

planting.note: Propagate by stem cuttings or divisions in spring.

directions: Grow this self-heading selection in a well-drained mix in bright indirect light to keep the pinstripe variegation crisp. Water when the top inch dries and wipe the leaves occasionally to keep them clean. Prized for glossy dark leaves finely striped in creamy white.

planting.note: Root nodal cuttings in the warm season.

directions: Provide bright indirect light to bring out the pink variegation and grow in a chunky, well-drained mix. Let the top inch dry between waterings and support the climbing stems on a moss pole. Grown entirely for its dark leaves splashed with bubblegum-pink; prune to balance variegation.

planting.note: Take stem cuttings with a node in spring or summer.

directions: Grow this climber in a well-drained mix with a moss pole for support and give it bright indirect light. Water when the top inch dries and keep it warm year-round. The arrowhead leaves and reddish stems make it a handsome foliage plant; mature specimens occasionally push deep red spathes.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring for new plants.

directions: Plant in a well-drained, fertile mix in bright indirect light or morning sun and keep evenly moist in warm weather. This compact, mounding selection needs no support and forms a tidy clump of deeply lobed leaves. Grown for its lush foliage rather than its infrequent inflorescences.

planting.note: Root rhizome cuttings laid on the mix surface in spring.

directions: Grow this crawler in a wide, shallow pot of chunky mix with the rhizome resting on the surface. Provide bright indirect light and high humidity, watering when the top layer dries. Prized for large velvety heart-shaped leaves with pale, contrasting veins.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring when repotting.

directions: Grow this self-heading hybrid in a well-drained mix in bright indirect light to encourage the brightest new-leaf color. Water when the top inch dries and avoid soggy soil. New leaves emerge coppery orange and age to green, forming a colorful rosette without needing support.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring to propagate.

directions: Plant in a rich, well-drained mix and give bright indirect light to keep the new growth glowing chartreuse. Water when the surface begins to dry and feed lightly through the growing season. This mounding hybrid needs no stake and is grown for its bright lime-green emerging leaves.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring when repotting.

directions: Grow this robust self-header in a well-drained mix in bright indirect light and water when the top inch dries. New leaves emerge burgundy-red and mature to glossy dark green on sturdy red stems. It forms a bold, upright clump with no need for support.

planting.note: Propagate from nodal stem cuttings in spring or summer.

directions: Root a cutting with a node and grow in a well-drained mix in bright indirect light to keep the foliage a vivid yellow-green. Let the top inch dry between waterings and pinch to encourage fullness. Grown as a trailing or climbing vine for its bright heart-shaped leaves.

planting.note: Root a stem cutting with a node in spring or summer.

directions: Root a nodal cutting, then pot into a chunky, well-drained mix and provide a moss pole for the climbing stems. Give bright indirect light and water when the top inch or two dries. Mature plants develop the classic split and holed leaves, and in ideal conditions produce a creamy spathe followed by an edible fruiting spadix.

planting.note: Propagate by nodal cuttings; this stable variegate grows slowly.

directions: Grow in a chunky, well-drained mix with a moss pole and give bright indirect light to support the creamy variegation. Water when the top inch or two dries, since variegated tissue is prone to rot if kept soggy. Grown for split leaves marbled and speckled with buttery cream.

planting.note: Propagate only by cutting, keeping a node with variegated growth.

directions: Give this unstable variegate bright indirect light and a very well-drained, chunky mix with a moss pole for support. Water carefully when the top inch dries, as the white sectors rot easily if overwatered. Grown for its striking green-and-white split leaves; prune to balance green and variegated growth.

planting.note: Root nodal cuttings and set against a flat support.

directions: Grow against a flat board or slab in a well-drained mix so the juvenile leaves press flat like shingles. Give bright indirect light and high humidity, watering when the surface dries. As it climbs, leaves enlarge and develop fenestrations, changing dramatically from the flat silver-marked juvenile stage.

planting.note: Set the tuber into a submerged planting basket once the pond water warms in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome at a shallow angle in heavy loam-filled aquatic baskets, with the growing tip exposed, and top with gravel to keep soil from clouding the water. Lower the basket so the crown sits four to twelve inches below the surface, deepening as leaves grow. Feed with aquatic tabs monthly and expect large garnet-red cups to open on sunny mornings from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Divide and pot the tuber in spring as the water begins to warm.

directions: Press the horizontal rhizome into clay loam with the crown just above the soil surface and cap with pea gravel. Position the container so the top of the pot rests six to eighteen inches under water. This reliable old variety blooms canary-yellow with mottled leaves; remove yellowing pads to keep water clear through the summer bloom season.

planting.note: Plant in spring after any risk of a hard freeze has passed.

directions: Bed the rhizome into a wide aquatic basket of dense soil, leaving the pink growing tip clear, and mulch the surface with gravel. Submerge so the crown sits eight to eighteen inches deep. Fragrant, star-white blooms with golden centers appear from early summer; deadhead spent flowers below the water to keep the pond tidy.

planting.note: A vigorous grower best planted in spring in a large pond.

directions: Give this strong grower a large, heavy container of clay soil and plant the tuber horizontally near the pot rim. Sink it so the crown rests twelve to thirty inches beneath the surface in a big pond where it has room to spread. Broad white blooms with crinkled petals float among huge leaves through the warm months.

planting.note: Compact enough for tubs and small ponds; plant in spring.

directions: Nestle the rhizome into a firm soil basket and cover with gravel, keeping the tip clear. Lower it so the crown sits six to eighteen inches down; this cultivar tolerates a bit of shade better than most. Cup-shaped rose-red flowers with a peppery scent open above bronze-mottled pads all summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a classic vigorous red for medium to large ponds.

directions: Set the tuber into a broad basket of heavy loam with the crown just proud of the soil, then gravel the top. Submerge twelve to twenty-four inches deep. Large, wine-red blooms with orange-tipped stamens hold their color well and open freely from midsummer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; suits medium ponds and half-barrels.

directions: Bed the rhizome in a soil-filled aquatic pot and top-dress with gravel, keeping the growing point uncovered. Sink the container so the crown sits six to eighteen inches under water. Fully double, snowball-white blooms with many packed petals appear through the summer over apple-green pads.

planting.note: Plant in spring in medium ponds; prized for fragrance.

directions: Anchor the rhizome in dense clay soil within a wide basket and finish with gravel. Position it so the crown rests eight to eighteen inches deep. Star-shaped, deep rose-pink flowers with incurving petals give off a strong anise scent from early summer onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring; also sold as 'Darwin'.

directions: Lay the tuber into a heavy soil basket with the tip exposed and cap with gravel. Submerge so the crown is eight to twenty inches deep. Full, many-petaled blooms open pale pink and deepen toward the center as they age, flowering generously through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; one of the longest-blooming white hardies.

directions: Set the rhizome in a firm soil-filled pot, leaving the crown clear, and gravel the surface. Lower so the crown sits six to eighteen inches down. Soft-white, star-form flowers with a faint blush open early each season and continue reliably later than most white varieties.

planting.note: A true miniature ideal for tubs and container ponds; plant in spring.

directions: Plant the small tuber in a shallow, wide pot of loam and cover lightly with gravel. Sink it so the crown sits just four to ten inches deep, making it perfect for a patio water bowl. Tiny yellow star flowers rise above olive pads mottled with maroon all summer long.

planting.note: Small red hardy for tubs and small ponds; plant in spring.

directions: Bed the tuber in dense soil in a compact basket and gravel over the top, keeping the tip clear. Submerge so the crown is four to twelve inches deep. Deep burgundy-red, cup-shaped blooms open freely and make this a dependable choice for smaller water gardens.

planting.note: An award-winning yellow hardy; plant in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome in a large basket of heavy loam and finish with gravel, leaving the crown exposed. Lower so the crown sits eight to twenty inches deep. Fragrant, star-form yellow blooms held slightly above the water appear in generous numbers over lightly speckled leaves.

planting.note: Free-flowering fragrant yellow; plant in spring.

directions: Set the rhizome into firm soil in a wide aquatic pot and top with gravel. Submerge so the crown sits six to eighteen inches deep. Fragrant, pale-yellow star flowers stand a little above the surface and bloom prolifically from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Salmon-peach hardy that blooms well in cooler water; plant in spring.

directions: Bed the tuber in heavy loam in a wide basket and cap with gravel, keeping the tip clear. Lower so the crown is six to eighteen inches under water. Warm salmon-pink blooms are held above the pads and keep coming through the season, even in partly shaded ponds.

planting.note: Peachy changeable hardy; plant in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome in a soil-filled basket, gravel the surface, and keep the crown exposed. Submerge so the crown sits six to eighteen inches deep. Blooms open a soft grapefruit-pink with apricot tones that shift as the flower ages over several days.

planting.note: Small red hardy for tubs and small ponds; plant in spring.

directions: Nestle the small tuber in dense soil in a compact pot and cover with gravel. Sink so the crown sits four to twelve inches deep, suiting patio ponds and half barrels. Rich red, cup-shaped flowers open freely over green pads through the summer months.

planting.note: Fragrant pink hardy noted for red-tipped stamens; plant in spring.

directions: Set the rhizome in firm loam in a wide basket and gravel over the top. Submerge so the crown is six to eighteen inches deep. Fragrant lilac-pink blooms are distinguished by fiery orange-red stamen tips and appear steadily through the warm season.

planting.note: Very dark red hardy; plant in spring.

directions: Plant the tuber in heavy soil in a broad basket, cap with gravel, and keep the growing tip clear. Lower so the crown rests six to eighteen inches deep. Deep blackish-red blooms with golden centers glow against green foliage and open across the summer.

planting.note: Award-winning speckled hardy with mottled foliage; plant in spring.

directions: Bed the rhizome in dense loam in a wide pot and finish with gravel. Submerge so the crown sits six to eighteen inches deep. Peachy-orange blooms are flecked and streaked with cream, and the marbled young leaves add extra interest through summer.

planting.note: Vivid deep-pink hardy; plant in spring.

directions: Set the tuber in heavy soil in a large basket and gravel the surface, keeping the tip exposed. Lower so the crown is eight to twenty inches deep. Large, brilliant fuchsia-pink flowers with a light fragrance open above green pads throughout the summer.

planting.note: Free-flowering pink hardy for medium ponds; plant in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome in a soil-filled basket, top with gravel, and keep the crown clear. Submerge so the crown sits six to eighteen inches deep. Pink, many-petaled fragrant blooms streaked with deeper rose appear abundantly from early summer to fall.

planting.note: Changeable hardy that shifts color over several days; plant in spring.

directions: Bed the tuber in firm loam in a wide pot and gravel over. Lower so the crown is six to eighteen inches deep. Blooms open soft yellow and deepen through apricot to coppery-orange over their life, giving a multicolored display on mottled foliage.

planting.note: Classic changeable orange hardy; plant in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome in heavy soil in a broad basket and finish with gravel. Submerge so the crown sits six to eighteen inches deep. Flowers emerge apricot-yellow and mature to a rich burnt orange, held above purple-flecked young pads through the season.

planting.note: Small changeable hardy ideal for tubs; plant in spring.

directions: Nestle the small tuber in dense soil in a compact basket and cover with gravel. Sink so the crown sits four to twelve inches deep, making it well suited to small water features. Blossoms open creamy yellow and change through orange to deep red over three days.

planting.note: North American native for natural ponds; plant rhizomes in spring.

directions: Plant the creeping rhizome horizontally in a heavy soil basket or directly into pond-bottom muck, keeping the tip exposed. Submerge the crown roughly twelve to thirty inches deep in still or slow water. Sweetly fragrant white blooms open each morning and close by afternoon from late spring through summer; this native spreads readily, so contain it in smaller ponds.

planting.note: Vigorous European native for large ponds and lakes; plant in spring.

directions: Set the rhizome into heavy loam in a large basket or the pond floor, leaving the growing tip clear. Sink so the crown rests eighteen to thirty-six inches deep, as this vigorous species prefers deeper water. Broad white cups with golden stamens float among large round leaves through summer; give it plenty of room to spread.

planting.note: Yellow-flowered species from the southern US and Mexico; plant in spring.

directions: Plant the tuber and its runner-forming rhizomes in a soil basket and cover with gravel. Submerge the crown about twelve to twenty-four inches deep in warm, sunny water. Bright yellow, slightly cupped flowers rise just above the mottled pads; this species spreads by runners, so contain it where you do not want it to roam.

planting.note: The smallest hardy species, perfect for tubs; plant in spring.

directions: Plant the tiny tuber in a shallow pot of loam and top with a thin gravel layer. Sink so the crown sits just three to eight inches deep, ideal for a small container pond. Little white star flowers barely an inch or two across open over small green pads through the warm months.

planting.note: Tropical species; plant only after water is reliably warm.

directions: Plant the tuber in a warm, soil-filled aquatic pot and cover with gravel once water holds above seventy degrees. Submerge the crown six to eighteen inches deep in full sun. Fragrant pale-blue, star-shaped flowers held above the water open in daylight; lift and store the tuber over winter where frost threatens.

planting.note: Night-blooming tropical species; plant after water warms.

directions: Plant the tuber in warm, heavy soil in an aquatic basket and finish with gravel. Submerge the crown six to eighteen inches deep in full sun to part shade. Large white to pink night-blooming flowers open in the evening over toothed, often reddish leaves; protect from frost or overwinter the tuber indoors.

planting.note: Large blue Australian tropical; plant in warm water only.

directions: Plant the tuber in a large, warm soil basket and gravel over the top. Submerge the crown twelve to twenty-four inches deep in a big pond in full sun. Large sky-blue, day-blooming flowers stand well above the water over broad pads; this tropical needs consistently warm conditions and frost protection.

planting.note: Deep-purple day-blooming tropical; plant after frost when water is warm.

directions: Plant the tuber in a warm soil basket and cover with gravel once the water holds above seventy degrees. Submerge the crown six to eighteen inches deep in full sun. Deep violet-purple flowers with golden centers stand above the pads and open by day; overwinter the tuber indoors in cold climates.

planting.note: Viviparous purple tropical; plant after frost in warm water.

directions: Plant the tuber in warm, rich soil in an aquatic pot and top with gravel. Submerge the crown six to eighteen inches deep in full sun. Reddish-purple, fragrant day flowers are freely produced, and this viviparous variety forms new plantlets on its leaves that can be potted up.

planting.note: Classic fragrant blue day-blooming tropical; plant in warm water.

directions: Plant the tuber in a large, warm soil basket and cover with gravel. Submerge the crown eight to twenty inches deep in full sun. Fragrant, large sky-blue flowers with dark-flecked leaves open by day and are held well above the water; lift the tuber before frost in cool regions.

planting.note: Compact viola-purple tropical good for smaller ponds; plant after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber in a warm soil pot and gravel the top. Submerge the crown six to fifteen inches deep in full sun. Violet-blue to purple, cup-shaped day flowers bloom freely on this compact and reliable tropical; store the tuber indoors over winter in cold climates.

planting.note: Compact blue tropical that blooms in cooler water than most; plant after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber in a warm soil basket and cover with gravel. Submerge the crown six to fifteen inches deep in full sun. Fragrant blue-purple day flowers appear reliably on this compact variety, which tolerates slightly cooler water than most tropicals and is often used in container ponds.

planting.note: Viviparous small blue tropical, excellent for tubs; plant after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber in a warm soil pot and gravel over. Submerge the crown four to twelve inches deep; this variety flowers even in part shade. Fragrant light-blue day blooms are held above the pads, and plantlets form where the leaf stalk meets the pad, making it easy to propagate.

planting.note: Lavender-blue viviparous tropical, good indoors too; plant after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber in a warm soil basket and top with gravel. Submerge the crown six to eighteen inches deep in full sun. Fragrant lavender-blue day flowers bloom prolifically and last several days; this viviparous variety tolerates greenhouse growing and forms plantlets on its leaves.

planting.note: Large white day-blooming tropical; plant after frost in warm water.

directions: Plant the tuber in a large warm soil basket and cover with gravel. Submerge the crown eight to twenty inches deep in full sun. Very large creamy-white, fragrant day flowers with pale-yellow stamens are held above the water and open freely; overwinter the tuber indoors where frost occurs.

planting.note: Large fragrant pink day-blooming tropical; plant after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber in a warm soil basket and gravel over the top. Submerge the crown eight to twenty inches deep in full sun. Large, strongly fragrant orchid-pink flowers stay open into the afternoon longer than many tropicals and bloom over a long season; lift the tuber before frost.

planting.note: Multicolored tropical that blooms in cooler water; plant after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber in a warm soil basket and top with gravel. Submerge the crown six to eighteen inches deep in full sun. Flowers blend pink, orange, and yellow tones, and this tropical is valued for blooming in cooler water and over a long season on handsomely mottled foliage.

planting.note: Night-blooming red tropical; plant after frost in warm water.

directions: Plant the tuber in a large warm soil basket and cover with gravel. Submerge the crown eight to twenty inches deep in full sun. Deep red, star-shaped flowers open in the evening and stay open into the morning over maroon foliage; this night bloomer is tender and must be overwintered indoors.

planting.note: Large white night-blooming tropical; plant after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber in a large warm soil basket and finish with gravel. Submerge the crown eight to twenty-four inches deep in full sun. Very large creamy-white flowers open in the evening and remain open into the next morning; this vigorous night bloomer is tender and needs indoor storage over winter.

planting.note: Deep-red night-blooming tropical; plant after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber in a warm soil basket and cover with gravel. Submerge the crown eight to twenty inches deep in full sun. Rich brick-red to deep-red flowers open in the evening over bronzy leaves and stay open into the morning; keep this tender night bloomer frost-free and store the tuber indoors in winter.

planting.note: Blue-purple mottled-leaf day tropical; plant after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber in a warm soil basket and gravel over the top. Submerge the crown six to eighteen inches deep in full sun. Purple-blue, fragrant day flowers are held above heavily speckled young leaves and open freely through the warm season; overwinter the tuber indoors in frost-prone areas.

planting.note: Set rhizomes in late summer or fall so roots establish before bloom season.

directions: Plant rhizomes just below the soil surface in rich, constantly moist ground or in a few inches of standing water at the pond edge. Keep the crown wet through summer and top-dress with compost in fall. Look for the deep velvety purple-black blooms in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Divide and replant rhizomes in late summer while soil stays warm and moist.

directions: Press the rhizome shallowly into wet, fertile soil and mulch to keep the roots cool and damp. Feed in early spring as new fans emerge and never let the soil dry out. The rich rusty-red flowers open in spring atop tall, sturdy stems.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in fall; this species is among the most cold-hardy Louisiana irises.

directions: Set rhizomes just below the surface in moist, rich soil where their short zigzag stems tuck among taller marginals. Keep evenly wet and divide crowded clumps every few years. Blue-violet flowers open in late spring on the characteristic bent stems.

planting.note: Divide and plant rhizomes in spring for cream-striped spring foliage.

directions: Plant the rhizome in a basket at the pond margin in shallow water or in constantly wet soil. The green-and-cream striped leaves are brightest in spring and fade toward green by summer. Yellow flowers follow in late spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in shallow standing water for reliable blooms.

directions: This true aquatic iris grows happily with its crown submerged in a few inches of water. Keep it flooded year-round and divide clumps after flowering. Broad blue flowers open in early summer above smooth, ribless leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, acidic soil kept wet through the growing season.

directions: Grow in fertile, lime-free soil that stays flooded in summer but drains in winter, or plant at a pond margin. Feed generously and keep saturated during bud formation. Large flat-topped flowers in purple, blue, or white open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shallow water at the margin of a pond.

directions: Grow this white-flowered form in a basket in a few inches of standing water with full sun. Trim spent stems to tidy the clump and divide every few years. Clean white flower spikes appear above heart-shaped foliage through summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers or divisions in spring in shallow water or mud.

directions: Press tubers into saturated mud or plant in a basket under a few inches of water. Also called duck potato, it forms edible tubers and spreads to fill a margin. Whorls of small three-petaled white flowers appear through summer above arrow-shaped leaves.

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring in shallow water at the pond edge.

directions: Set the tubers into a submerged basket in a few inches of water in full sun. This double form holds its blooms longer than the single species and spreads more slowly. Rosette-like double white flowers open in mid to late summer above bold arrowhead leaves.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring once water has warmed for strong summer growth.

directions: Grow this bold marginal in a large basket submerged in up to a foot of water. The paddle leaves have a chalky powder-blue coating and rise on tall stalks. Dangling violet flowers on arching stems appear above the foliage in summer.
